read-tracked-and-untracked-state.label: Read the Repository State
read-tracked-and-untracked-state.summary: Use full and short status views to distinguish untracked files, tracked edits, and changes already prepared for a commit.

try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.title: Recognize an Untracked File
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `event-board/` repository.

In `event-board/`, use short status to identify the new `schedule.md` file before it has ever been staged.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: An untracked file exists in the working tree but is not yet part of Git’s index or history.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf event-board
mkdir -p event-board
cd event-board
printf %s '# Event Schedule
' > schedule.md
cd ..
git -C event-board init -q -b main
git -C event-board config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C event-board config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf event-board
mkdir -p event-board
cd event-board
printf %s '# Event Schedule
' > schedule.md
cd ..
git -C event-board init -q -b main
git -C event-board config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C event-board config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"

try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.instructions:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `event-board/` repository.

In `event-board/`, use short status to identify the new `schedule.md` file before it has ever been staged.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

0.message: Do not use destructive history-rewriting commands in this course.
Run `git status --short`, then check your answer again.

try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.title: Recognize a Staged File
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `event-board/` repository.

`schedule.md` is already staged for the first snapshot. Use short status and a staged-name listing to verify that it is in the index.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: A staged file is in the index and will be included in the next commit unless the staging area changes.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf event-board
mkdir -p event-board
cd event-board
printf %s '# Event Schedule
' > schedule.md
cd ..
git -C event-board init -q -b main
git -C event-board config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C event-board config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
git -C event-board add -- schedule.md

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf event-board
mkdir -p event-board
cd event-board
printf %s '# Event Schedule
' > schedule.md
cd ..
git -C event-board init -q -b main
git -C event-board config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C event-board config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"

try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.instructions:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `event-board/` repository.

`schedule.md` is already staged for the first snapshot. Use short status and a staged-name listing to verify that it is in the index.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

0.message: Do not use destructive history-rewriting commands in this course.
Run `git status --short`, then check your answer again.

1.message: Run `git diff --staged --name-only`, then check your answer again.

try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.title: Recognize a Tracked Modification
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `event-board/` repository.

The board has one committed schedule, and its time has changed in the working tree. Use short status and `git diff` to inspect the unstaged edit.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: A tracked modification differs from the last committed snapshot but has not entered the staging area.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf event-board
mkdir -p event-board
git -C event-board init -q -b main
git -C event-board config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C event-board config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd event-board
printf %s 'Doors open at 9 AM.
' > schedule.md
cd ..
git -C event-board add -A
git -C event-board commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd event-board
printf %s 'Doors open at 10 AM.
' > schedule.md
cd ..

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf event-board
mkdir -p event-board
git -C event-board init -q -b main
git -C event-board config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C event-board config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd event-board
printf %s 'Doors open at 9 AM.
' > schedule.md
cd ..
git -C event-board add -A
git -C event-board commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd event-board
printf %s 'Doors open at 10 AM.
' > schedule.md
cd ..

try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.title: Confirm a Clean Working Tree
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `event-board/` repository.

The current event-board snapshot is committed and unchanged. Use full and short status to confirm that there is nothing left to stage or commit.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: A clean working tree means the files, index, and current commit agree.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

single-choice-1.title: Purpose of git status
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the `git status` command?
single-choice-1.hint: Think about what information `git status` provides about your repository.

help.concept: `git status` provides information about the current state of the working directory and staging area.
help.hint_1: It shows which changes are staged, unstaged, or untracked.
help.hint_2: It helps you understand what will be included in the next commit.

options.a: To commit changes to the repository
options.b: To show the current branch and changes
options.d: To delete files from the repository

single-choice-2.title: Understanding git status --short
single-choice-2.prompt: What does the `??` symbol indicate in the output of `git status --short`?
single-choice-2.hint: Consider what `??` might mean about a file's status in the repository.

help.concept: The `??` symbol indicates untracked files in `git status --short`.
help.hint_1: Untracked files are those not yet added to the staging area.
help.hint_2: `??` means the file is new and not tracked by Git.

multi-choice-1.title: Components of git status output
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which of the following are components of the `git status` output? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-1.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: Git status distinguishes files Git has never tracked from tracked files that are modified or staged.
help.hint_1: Compare the question to the lesson example and remove details that do not match the requested concept.
help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

drag-reorder-1.title: Order of git status sections
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the sections of the `git status` output in the correct order.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Read each piece and arrange them in the order the statement should be understood.

help.concept: The pieces should form a valid statement in a logical order.
help.hint_1: Start with the piece that introduces the idea or action.
help.hint_2: Place dependent pieces after the part they describe or complete.

fill-blank-choice-1.title: Symbol for modified files
fill-blank-choice-1.prompt: In `git status --short`, the symbol for a modified file is `[blank1]`.
fill-blank-choice-1.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: Git status distinguishes files Git has never tracked from tracked files that are modified or staged.
help.hint_1: Think about what the missing part needs to do in the statement.
help.hint_2: Choose the term that best completes the meaning of the statement.

fill-blank-choice-1.template: In `git status --short`, the symbol for a modified file is `[blank1]`.

fill-blank-choice-2.title: Symbol for untracked files
fill-blank-choice-2.prompt: In `git status --short`, the symbol for an untracked file is `[blank1]`.
fill-blank-choice-2.hint: Focus on the missing concept rather than the exact missing term.

sketch0.title: Let Git Describe the Folder
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
`git status` is the safest first question in an unfamiliar repository. It reports the current branch, staged changes, unstaged changes, and untracked files in plain language.

```bash
git status
```

Suppose `outline.md` is new and `README.md` was edited. The report separates those facts instead of treating every file the same. Nothing changes when you run status; it is an inspection command.

Use the full view while learning because its headings explain where each change lives.

sketch1.title: Read the Two Status Columns
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
The short view compresses the same information into two columns:

```bash
git status --short
```

A few common shapes are:

```text
?? outline.md
 M README.md
M  schedule.md
```

`??` means untracked. A mark in the right column means the working file differs from the staged version. A mark in the left column means a change is already staged for the next commit. The spacing is meaningful, so read both columns.

sketch2.title: Tracked and Untracked Are Different
sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
A tracked file already belongs to repository history. Git can compare its current contents with an earlier snapshot. An untracked file exists in the folder, but Git has no recorded version to compare.

Staging a new file introduces it to Git:

```bash
git add outline.md
git status --short
```

The status changes from `?? outline.md` to `A  outline.md`. `A` means the new file is prepared for the next commit. The file is not committed yet; staging and committing are separate decisions.

sketch3.title: Check Before You Change State
sketch3.bodyMarkdown:
A reliable Git rhythm is inspect, act, inspect again. Before staging, run status. After staging, run it again. After committing, confirm the working tree is clean.

This habit catches the mistakes that command memorization misses: operating in the wrong folder, staging an unrelated file, or assuming a change was committed when it was only staged.

When status surprises you, stop and read it. Git is describing the state it will grade and eventually record.
